design_philosophy.core_loop: Rail-based shooting: aim and fire at enemies/vehicles with limited ammo, manage resource constraints, progress through stages by meeting kill counter objectives, rescue hostages as narrative goal
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (ammunition, grenades) balanced against enemy density and incoming projectile threats; dual tension between offensive capability and defensive survival
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Elite special forces operative (Roy Adams) conducting surgical military rescue missions; first-person gunner experiencing arcade-style combat with weapon feedback (arcade cabinet featured recoil-simulating gun controller)
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Uncompromising rail shooter design: players have no movement control, only aim/fire agency; mandatory hostage rescue objective creates moral/mechanical constraint (friendly fire damages player); game rewards completionist play (rescue all hostages for better ending) while permitting speedrun paths; stage order unlocks vary by region (Japan allows player choice; US/EU enforce sequence)

difficulty.lives_system: Damage bar system: health depletes from enemy attacks or friendly fire; fills across stage completion
difficulty.continues_system: Continue system available; restarting stage rather than full game reset
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based checkpointing; failure to rescue single hostage triggers game over; multiple ending screens vary by rescue count and player performance
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited direct difficulty selection; player agency expressed through stage order choice (Japan version) and hostage rescue completion target (affects ending quality and score bonus); resource management of ammo/grenades creates dynamic difficulty tension

progression.upgrade_system: Temporary power-ups within stages: special machine gun (unlimited ammo + increased fire rate for 10 seconds), dynamite bombs (screen-clear damage), health recovery items; no permanent upgrades across stages
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stage progression unlocked narratively: jungle stage → soldier interrogation reveals concentration camp location → next stage unlocks; Prison Camp and Airport have prerequisites; Japanese version allows non-linear mission order while maintaining narrative dependencies
replayability_hooks: Multiple endings based on hostage rescue count (NES version specific)
Regional variation in stage order (Japan has free choice vs. US/EU forced sequence)
Scoring/bonus system rewards complete hostage rescue vs. minimal objectives
Stage-specific objectives and effects create varied gameplay scenarios

world_building_techniques: First-person perspective establishes player embodiment as Special Forces operative Roy Adams
Enemy soldier interrogation sequences advance plot exposition (reveals location of next stage)
Stage environments (jungle, concentration camp, village, airport) provide implicit military campaign context
Hostage rescue mechanic embedded in core gameplay—civilians appear as friendly targets, friendly fire penalty reinforces rescue objective thematically
Civilian types differentiate stage identity (women, children; bikini babe in Japanese village stage)
Game over screens vary contextually (death vs. hostage loss) providing narrative feedback

faction_indicators: Player role: Special Forces operative (allied military)
Enemy forces: Military soldiers, vehicles (trucks, boats, helicopters, armored transports), bazooka/mortar teams
Civilians: Hostages requiring rescue (women, children, cultural variants)
Stage locations suggest geopolitical conflict: jungle (implied tropical staging), concentration camp (authoritarian regime), village (civilian infrastructure), airport (strategic asset)
danger_signals: Enemy projectile types escalate (gunfire → grenades → mortar → bazooka → missiles) indicating increasing threat sophistication
Friendly fire mechanic creates ethical constraint: player must distinguish and spare civilians
Stage counter mechanic enforces kill quota—players must eliminate specific enemy numbers to advance
Damage accumulation from enemy contact or collateral damage creates attrition pressure
environmental_storytelling: Jungle terrain → implied remote/tropical military operation location
Concentration camp → authoritarian regime, captive hostage scenario
Village with civilian presence → collateral damage risk, rescue mission complexity
Airport stage → strategic extraction point, final objective location
Progressive stage environments establish military campaign narrative arc (infiltration → extraction)
